era: 1960-1968 core. Garage Rock. E=8 -- proto-punk maximum. Tacoma. Etiquette Records.
associated_musicians: Paul Revere and the Raiders (PNW garage era)
The Kingsmen (Richard Berry connection via Have Love Will Travel)

theory_nodes.concept: E=8 ties engine maximum. The Sonics are the proto-punk progenitor -- the loudest, most aggressive band of the 1960s PNW. Gerry Roslie's screaming vocals predate punk by a decade. 'Psycho' (1964) is the founding document of horror-punk, proto-punk, and extreme garage rock.
theory_nodes.description: E=8 at degree 241 -- ties Blood Brothers at 218. Garage era peak.

theory_nodes.concept: Northwest Sound defined. Etiquette Records (Buck Ormsby, 1960), Joe Boles studio, Tacoma-Seattle ecosystem. Raw, organ-heavy, distorted -- a regional variation on garage rock shaped by geographic isolation from national trend-setters.
theory_nodes.description: The Etiquette Records / Northwest Sound ecosystem.

theory_nodes.concept: Proto-punk genealogy: Sonics (1964) → Stooges (1969) → Ramones (1974) → Cramps (covered Psycho) → White Stripes (Jack White citation). The influence chain is direct and documented. The Sonics invented extreme rock before anyone named it.
theory_nodes.description: The Sonics' direct lineage to punk, post-punk, and garage revival.

cross_references.relationship: Only other E=8 in the engine. Post-hardcore screaming vs. proto-punk screaming. Four decades between them; same energy reading.
PNW garage era contemporaries. E=6. More commercial. The polished version of the same decade.
Tacoma garage predecessors. The Wailers established the instrumental tradition the Sonics pushed to its vocal extreme.
Both recorded Richard Berry material: Sonics covered 'Have Love Will Travel'; Kingsmen covered 'Louie Louie'. Berry links the whole era.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=8 vs E=2 -- spread of 6, among the widest in the engine. The loudest band of the 1960s PNW paired with the quietest fish of the glacial stream. Maximum noise; maximum silence.
